author: orion3336
content:
On performance: 5ms median orchestration overhead per task, which is <0.1% assuming 7+ seconds proof time. The bigger win from Rust-native is avoiding serialization of large intermediate artifacts when composing proofs in-process.

On reliability: push0 comes with built-in fault tolerance relying on ack and progress (at-least-once delivery) and has been running Zircuit's production proving pipeline since March 2025, processing over 14 million mainnet blocks.

On direction: I'm a bit biased haha but I lean more towards Rust-native for type safety and serialization overhead. Would be happy to lend a hand benchmarking push0 vs whatever ethproofs is considering to use.

On off-the-shelf vs bespoke: ZK proofs are a whole different animal than AI pipelines. Specialization opens room for more optimization but feature parity is a real concern. Perhaps a stepping stone is to compile a requirement set and shop around. Here are push0's requirements from the paper based on our experience:

author: taulepton_
content:
> The bigger win from Rust-native is avoiding serialization of large intermediate artifacts when composing proofs in-process.

Is this something that is exclusively possible with rust-native orchestration? If I'm not mistaken a lot of python libraries (e.g. numpy) delegate the intensive compute / memory to a C backend. I would assume that a similar pattern can be achieved with rust bindings?

> On off-the-shelf vs bespoke: ZK proofs are a whole different animal than AI pipelines. 

Could you elaborate on this please? From my experience working with Ray, it provides core primitives (Tasks, Actors, Objects) that define your distrubuted context and you can build whatever you want using them. In many cases it is data processing or model training / inference  that is build on top but its not the only thing that can be done. From a high level we have some process (proof generation) that needs to be distributed across a cluster of GPU's and CPU's in an efficient, reliable and robust way. Imo this is very similar to AI workflows even if the implementation specifics differ. 

> Would be happy to lend a hand benchmarking push0 vs whatever ethproofs is considering to use.

It would be great if you could define a framework to perform the benchmarking. It's interesting actually because the performance of different orchestration solutions may differ based on the environement (number of hosts, GPU's, CPU's etc).

> Perhaps a stepping stone is to compile a requirement set and shop around. Here are push0's requirements from the paper based on our experience.

This sounds like a very useful step.
